passages.title: The Computer That Learned to Play
passages.text:
In March 2016, the world watched an unusual competition. Lee Sedol, one of the best players of the ancient board game Go, sat across from an opponent that had no face, no hands, and no feelings. It was a computer program called AlphaGo, built by a team of scientists. Most people believed the human would win easily. Go has more possible moves than there are atoms in the universe, so computers were thought to be too limited to master it. To everyone's surprise, AlphaGo won four games out of five.

How did a machine beat a human champion? The secret is a method called machine learning. Instead of following fixed rules written by programmers, AlphaGo studied millions of past games and played against itself again and again. Every time it made a mistake, it adjusted itself, just like a basketball player who practices free throws after missing a shot. In a few months, the program played more games than any human could in several lifetimes.

Today, artificial intelligence is doing much more than winning board games. AI helps doctors find illnesses in X-ray pictures, helps farmers decide when to water their crops, and even helps game designers create smarter enemies in video games. Some AI systems can chat with people, draw pictures, or translate languages in seconds.

However, scientists remind us that AI is a tool, not magic. It learns from the data humans give it, so it can also learn human mistakes. A face-recognition system trained mostly on photos of adults, for example, may fail to recognize children.

Many experts believe the future will not be "humans versus machines" but "humans with machines". Just as calculators did not end the need for math classes, AI will not replace human thinking. Instead, people who understand AI will have a powerful teammate — and perhaps the next great AI scientist is reading this article right now.

questions.q: What is the passage mainly about?
options: How AlphaGo defeated the world's best Go player
How AI learns from data and how it is changing our lives
Why the game of Go is harder than chess
Why machines will soon replace all human workers
questions.explanation: 主旨题。全文从 AlphaGo 引入机器学习的原理，再扩展到 AI 在医疗、农业、游戏等领域的应用，最后讨论人机协作的未来。B 概括最全面；A 只是开头的事例，C 文中未比较，D 与作者“AI 是工具而非替代者”的观点相反。

questions.q: Why did most people believe Lee Sedol would beat AlphaGo?
options: Because AlphaGo had never played a real game before
Because computers were not allowed in official Go matches
Because Go has so many possible moves that computers seemed unable to master it
Because Lee Sedol had never lost a match in his life
questions.explanation: 细节题。第一段明确说围棋的可能走法比宇宙原子还多，人们因此认为电脑无法掌握围棋，所以看好李世石。

questions.q: In paragraph 2, the word "adjusted" is closest in meaning to ___.
options: changed slightly in order to improve
stopped working completely
copied from another program
sold at a lower price
questions.explanation: 词义猜测题。上下文说 AlphaGo 每次犯错后都会“调整”自己，就像球员投丢后练习罚球，可见 adjusted 意为“做出调整以改进”。

questions.q: The face-recognition example in paragraph 4 shows that ___.
options: AI systems are too expensive for hospitals
AI can repeat human mistakes if it learns from limited data
children should not use AI products
face recognition is the most useful kind of AI
questions.explanation: 细节题。第四段说 AI 从人类给的数据中学习，因此也会学到人类的错误；只用成人照片训练的系统可能认不出儿童，正是“数据有局限就会犯错”的例子。

questions.q: What is the author's attitude toward AI?
options: Fearful, because AI will take away all jobs
Doubtful, because AI never works in real life
Positive, seeing AI as a useful teammate for humans
Angry, because AI beat a human champion
questions.explanation: 推断题。最后一段作者把 AI 比作计算器，认为它不会取代人类思维，而是“强大的队友”（powerful teammate），态度积极乐观。

passages.title: Journey into the Deep
passages.text:
The ocean covers about 70 percent of our planet, yet we know less about the deep sea than we do about the surface of the Moon. Sunlight can only reach about 200 meters down. Below that lies a world of complete darkness, freezing water, and pressure strong enough to crush a car like a soft drink can.

To visit this world, scientists use special machines. The most famous are deep-sea submersibles — small submarines built with thick metal walls. China's Fendouzhe and the American Alvin have carried scientists thousands of meters down. Many dives are done by robots called ROVs, which are controlled from a ship on the surface through a long cable.

What have explorers found down there? Plenty of surprises. There are underwater mountains taller than any on land, and valleys deeper than the Grand Canyon. Around hot springs on the ocean floor, where the water is heated by volcanoes, live strange creatures that never see sunlight. Instead of using sunlight to make food as plants do, tiny bacteria there use chemicals from the springs. These bacteria feed whole communities of giant tube worms, white crabs, and blind shrimps.

Many deep-sea animals make their own light, a skill called bioluminescence. Some use light to attract food, some to scare enemies, and some to find friends. If you could swim there, you would see flashes and glows like a night sky full of moving stars.

Deep-sea exploration is not just about adventure. Scientists study these creatures to look for new medicines, and they study the ocean floor to understand earthquakes better. Engineers also learn from deep-sea animals: the strong, light shells of some deep-sea creatures have inspired better designs for submarines.

The deep ocean still hides countless secrets. Every dive finds species nobody has ever seen before. Perhaps one day you will pilot a submersible yourself and discover a creature that the world will name after you.

questions.q: What makes the deep sea so difficult to explore?
options: It is too close to the Moon to reach
There is no sunlight, the water is freezing, and the pressure is extremely high
Ships are not allowed to sail there
The water is too hot because of volcanoes
questions.explanation: 细节题。第一段末尾列举了深海三大难题：完全黑暗、冰冷的海水和足以压扁汽车的高压。

questions.q: The word "bioluminescence" in paragraph 4 means ___.
options: a type of submarine used by scientists
the way plants use sunlight to make food
the ability of living things to produce their own light
a disease caused by deep-sea pressure
questions.explanation: 词义猜测题。第四段首句说许多深海动物“make their own light, a skill called bioluminescence”，即生物自己发光的能力（生物荧光）。

questions.q: Where do the bacteria near underwater hot springs get their energy?
options: From sunlight that reaches the ocean floor
From the fish they catch
From chemicals coming out of the hot springs
From cables connected to ships
questions.explanation: 细节题。第三段说这些细菌不像植物那样利用阳光，而是利用热泉喷出的化学物质获取能量。

questions.q: Why do engineers study the shells of deep-sea creatures?
options: They want to sell the shells as souvenirs
They hope to teach the creatures to make light
They need shells to repair underwater cables
They want to copy useful designs from nature to build better submarines
questions.explanation: 推断题。第五段说深海生物又轻又坚固的壳“启发了更好的潜艇设计”，即工程师向大自然借鉴设计（仿生学）。

questions.q: What is the main purpose of this passage?
options: To warn people about the dangers of submarines
To describe a dive that went wrong
To introduce the deep sea and explain why exploring it matters
To teach readers how to build an ROV
questions.explanation: 主旨题。文章介绍深海环境、探测工具、奇妙生物和深海探索的科学价值，目的是向读者介绍深海并说明探索的意义，而不是讲故事或教技术。

passages.title: A Hand Made of Plastic and Kindness
passages.text:
When 14-year-old Leo Carter saw his classmate Ben struggling to open his lunchbox, he had an idea. Ben was born without fingers on his left hand, and a medical artificial hand can cost as much as a family car. Leo wondered: could he build one himself?

Leo was not an engineer, but he was stubborn. He found free designs online, shared by a worldwide group of volunteers who make artificial hands for children. His school had a 3D printer, a machine that builds objects layer by layer from melted plastic, like squeezing toothpaste in careful lines. With the help of his science teacher, Leo printed the fingers, the palm, and the joints, one piece at a time.

The first hand was a disaster. The fingers broke after two days, and the strings that pulled them kept getting stuck. But engineers expect failure; they call each failure a "test". Leo changed the design, used stronger plastic, and replaced the thread with fishing line. The second hand worked better. The third version could hold a water bottle, catch a softball, and — most importantly for Ben — open a lunchbox.

The hand cost about 50 dollars in materials, less than one percent of the price of a medical one. It works simply: when Ben bends his wrist, cables inside the hand pull the fingers closed, like the strings on a puppet.

Leo's story spread online, and soon three other schools started similar projects. Engineers say this is how invention really works: notice a problem, build a solution, test it, fail, and improve it. Big companies use exactly the same method, just with bigger budgets.

Leo is now designing a hand that can play video games, with a special thumb for the controller. "Ben keeps beating me," Leo laughs, "so I guess the hand works perfectly."

questions.q: Why did Leo decide to build an artificial hand?
options: His teacher gave him a school project about hands
He wanted to win a science competition
His classmate needed one and medical hands were too expensive
His school had just bought a new 3D printer
questions.explanation: 细节题。第一段说 Leo 看到同学 Ben 打不开午餐盒，而医用假手贵得像一辆家用汽车，于是他萌生了自制的想法。

questions.q: In paragraph 2, the word "stubborn" is closest in meaning to ___.
options: good at making friends quickly
unable to understand machines
careless with expensive tools
refusing to give up even when things are difficult
questions.explanation: 词义猜测题。上下文说 Leo 不是工程师却坚持自学设计、打印零件，后面经历多次失败仍不断改进，可见 stubborn 这里是褒义：“倔强的、不肯放弃的”。

questions.q: How does the artificial hand close its fingers?
options: Batteries power a small motor in each finger
When the user bends his wrist, cables pull the fingers closed
A computer reads the user's brain signals
The fingers close automatically every few seconds
questions.explanation: 细节题。第四段解释了原理：当 Ben 弯曲手腕时，假手内部的线缆像木偶线一样把手指拉拢。

questions.q: What does Leo's story suggest about invention?
options: Only trained engineers can invent useful things
Inventions always succeed on the first try
Failure is a normal and useful part of the inventing process
Good inventions must be expensive
questions.explanation: 推断题。Leo 的第一只手是“灾难”，但工程师把每次失败叫作一次“测试”，不断改进才成功。可见失败是发明过程中正常且有用的一环。

questions.q: What is the best title for this passage?
options: How 3D Printers Work
A Hand Made of Plastic and Kindness
The History of Artificial Hands
Why Fishing Line Is Useful
questions.explanation: 主旨题。文章讲的是少年用 3D 打印的塑料手帮助同学的温暖故事，B 既点出材料（塑料）又点出情感（善意），最贴切；其余选项都只是文中的细节。

passages.title: Why Do Your Sneakers Cost So Much?
passages.text:
Walk into any sports shop and you will find sneakers that cost as much as a new smartphone. The materials — rubber, cloth, and plastic — are worth only a few dollars. So where does all the money go? The answer teaches us a lot about how business works.

First, there are the real costs. A shoe company pays factories to make the shoes, ships them across the ocean, and pays shops to sell them. Workers, designers, and delivery drivers all need to be paid. These costs explain part of the price, but only a small part.

The bigger part is something economists call "brand value". Companies spend millions on advertisements and on famous athletes who wear their shoes. When your favorite basketball star jumps high on TV, the company wants you to connect that feeling with its logo. You are not just buying rubber and cloth; you are buying a dream, and dreams are expensive.

Scarcity is another powerful force. Some companies make only a small number of special shoes. When many people want something that is rare, the price rises. This is the basic economic law of supply and demand. A limited-edition shoe can be resold for ten times its original price, just like a rare skin in a video game.

Understanding these ideas helps you become a smarter shopper. Before buying, you can ask yourself: am I paying for quality, or am I paying for a logo? Sometimes an expensive shoe really is better — it may protect your feet or last longer. Sometimes it is almost the same as a cheaper shoe made in the same factory.

Business itself is not a bad thing. Companies create jobs, and competition pushes them to make better products. The best customers, however, are those who know the tricks of the game. In the marketplace, just like in your favorite game, knowledge is power.

questions.q: What is the passage mainly about?
options: Why smartphones are cheaper than sneakers
What makes sneakers expensive and how to shop wisely
How to design your own sports shoes
The history of the shoe industry
questions.explanation: 主旨题。文章用球鞋价格讲解真实成本、品牌价值和供需规律，最后给出理性消费建议。B 全面概括了“为什么贵 + 怎么聪明消费”两条主线。

questions.q: In paragraph 4, the word "scarcity" means ___.
options: a kind of expensive material
the state of being rare or in short supply
a discount given to students
a factory that makes shoes
questions.explanation: 词义猜测题。第四段说公司只生产少量特别款球鞋，“很多人想要稀少的东西，价格就上涨”，可见 scarcity 意为“稀缺、稀少”。

questions.q: According to the law of supply and demand mentioned in the passage, prices rise when ___.
options: a factory produces too many products
shops stay open for longer hours
materials are easy to find
many people want something that is rare
questions.explanation: 细节题。第四段直接说明：当许多人想要某样稀少的东西时（需求大、供给少），价格就会上涨，这就是供需法则。

questions.q: What does the author suggest about expensive sneakers?
options: They are always of better quality than cheap ones
They are never worth buying
They may not always be better, so buyers should think before paying
They are made in different factories than cheap shoes
questions.explanation: 推断题。第五段说贵的鞋有时确实更好，有时却和同厂生产的便宜鞋几乎一样，所以作者建议买之前先想清楚自己是在为质量还是为 logo 买单。

questions.q: What does "brand value" mainly come from, according to the passage?
options: Better rubber and cloth
Advertising and famous athletes who wear the shoes
Higher factory wages
Longer shipping distances
questions.explanation: 细节题。第三段说公司花数百万做广告、请著名运动员穿鞋，让消费者把对球星的情感与品牌联系起来——这就是品牌价值的主要来源。

passages.title: Why "Just One More Game" Is Never Just One
passages.text:
It is 10:30 on a school night. You promised your parents — and yourself — that you would stop after one more round. Forty minutes later, you are still playing, and the homework on your desk remains untouched. If this sounds familiar, do not feel too guilty: you have simply met some of the most carefully designed psychological traps in the modern world, built by people who understand your brain better than you do.

Psychologists explain much of this with a chemical called dopamine. Often described as the "pleasure chemical", dopamine is actually more about expectation than pleasure. Your brain releases it not when you get a reward, but when you expect one. Game designers use this brilliantly. A treasure box that might contain a rare skin is far more exciting than one that certainly does, because an uncertain reward keeps the dopamine flowing. This is the same mechanism that makes slot machines so hard to walk away from.

Another powerful trick is the "goal-gradient effect": the closer you feel to a goal, the harder you push. That is why battle passes show you exactly how close you are to the next level, and why "one more match" feels reasonable when your rank bar is nearly full. Losses pull you in as well. After a defeat, your brain wants to fix the uncomfortable feeling immediately, and the fastest fix it knows is another game.

Understanding these designs does not mean games are evil or that you should never play. Games train reaction speed, teamwork, and strategic thinking, and playing with friends is a genuine social pleasure. The point is that the same brain that can be trapped can also learn to notice the trap. Research shows that simply being aware of these mechanisms makes people better at resisting them.

Psychologists suggest practical strategies: decide your stopping time before you start, keep the phone in another room while studying, and replace "I should stop" with a specific plan such as "when this round ends, I will stand up". Vague promises rarely beat clever design; specific plans often do.

Your attention is valuable — entire industries compete for it. Learning how your own mind works is the first step to making sure that you, not an algorithm, decide how you spend your nights.

questions.q: What is the passage mainly about?
options: Why video games should be banned for students
The psychological tricks that keep us playing, and how to take back control
How to design a successful video game
Why homework is more boring than games
questions.explanation: 主旨题。文章解释了多巴胺、目标梯度效应等让人停不下来的心理机制，并在后半部分给出应对策略，核心是“识破陷阱 + 夺回控制权”，B 概括最全面。

questions.q: In paragraph 2, the word "expectation" is closest in meaning to ___.
options: the strong feeling that something is about to happen
a prize you have already won
a mistake your brain makes
the memory of a past game
questions.explanation: 词义猜测题。第二段说大脑不是“得到奖励时”而是“期待奖励时”释放多巴胺，expectation 即“期待、预期某事即将发生的感觉”。

questions.q: According to the passage, an uncertain reward is more exciting because ___.
options: it is usually bigger than a certain reward
it is easier to win
it costs less money
it keeps the brain's dopamine flowing
questions.explanation: 细节题。第二段说“可能开出稀有皮肤的宝箱”比“一定有”的更刺激，因为不确定的奖励让多巴胺持续分泌——老虎机也是同样原理。

questions.q: The "goal-gradient effect" explains why ___.
options: battle passes show how close you are to the next level
players prefer single-player games
games become cheaper over time
some players quit after losing once
questions.explanation: 细节题。第三段说目标梯度效应是“越接近目标越努力”，所以通行证会精确显示你离下一级有多近，让你觉得“再来一局很合理”。

questions.q: What can we infer about the author's attitude toward video games?
options: Games are evil and should never be played
Games have real benefits, but players should understand the traps designed into them
Games are the only way to train the brain
Only adults should be allowed to play games
questions.explanation: 推断题。第四段明确说游戏能训练反应、团队合作和策略思维，与朋友开黑也是真正的社交乐趣；作者反对的是被设计好的“陷阱”控制，而非游戏本身。

passages.title: Cities Designed for People
passages.text:
Imagine two streets. On the first, six lanes of cars roar past, and crossing the road feels like a dangerous mission. On the second, trees shade wide sidewalks, children cycle safely in protected lanes, and small shops sell bread, books, and bubble tea. Both streets exist in modern cities, and the difference between them is not an accident — it is the result of urban planning, the science of designing how a city grows.

For most of the twentieth century, cities were designed mainly for cars. Planners built wide highways, huge parking lots, and neighborhoods where homes, schools, and shops were so far apart that driving became the only realistic choice. This seemed modern and efficient at the time, but it created serious problems: traffic jams that steal hours from people's days, air pollution that harms children's lungs, and lonely neighborhoods where nobody walks and neighbors never meet.

Today, a new idea is changing cities around the world: the "15-minute city". The concept, promoted by scientists and adopted in places like Paris and Melbourne, is simple — everything you need for daily life, such as school, food, parks, and doctors, should be reachable within fifteen minutes on foot or by bicycle. When daily needs are close, people drive less, exercise more, and spend more time with their families. Shopkeepers benefit too, because people walking past a shop are far more likely to enter it than people speeding past at sixty kilometers per hour.

Changing a city is harder than changing a video game map, of course. New bike lanes mean fewer parking spaces, which angers some drivers and shop owners who fear losing customers. Successful cities have learned to move carefully: they test changes on a few streets first, measure the results, and talk with the people who live there. In several cities, temporary "trial" bike lanes became permanent after data showed that accidents fell and local shops earned more money, not less.

The cities of the future are being designed right now, and their designers face a fascinating question: should a city be a machine for moving cars, or a home for raising humans? The students of today will answer that question — as engineers, as voters, and simply as people deciding where and how to live.

questions.q: What is the passage mainly about?
options: Why cars should be banned from all cities
How cities are shifting from car-centered to people-centered design
The history of highways in the twentieth century
How to open a shop on a busy street
questions.explanation: 主旨题。文章对比“为车设计”和“为人设计”的城市，介绍“15 分钟城市”理念和改造方法，主线是城市规划从以车为中心转向以人为中心。

questions.q: In paragraph 4, "trial" bike lanes are lanes that ___.
options: are only for professional cyclists
are built permanently from the start
are tested for a period before a final decision is made
are used for legal cases
questions.explanation: 词义猜测题。第四段说城市先在几条街上试验、测量结果，数据好了才把“trial”自行车道变成永久的，可见 trial 意为“试验性的、试行的”。

questions.q: Which of the following is NOT mentioned as a problem caused by car-centered city design?
options: Traffic jams
Air pollution
Lonely neighborhoods
High housing prices
questions.explanation: 细节题（排除题）。第二段列举了以车为中心带来的问题：堵车、空气污染、邻里孤独。高房价（D）文中并未提及，故选 D。

questions.q: In a "15-minute city", people should be able to ___.
options: drive to work in fifteen minutes
reach daily needs like school and food within fifteen minutes on foot or by bike
cross the whole city in fifteen minutes
build a house in fifteen minutes
questions.explanation: 细节题。第三段定义了“15 分钟城市”：学校、食物、公园、医生等日常生活所需，步行或骑车十五分钟内都能到达。

questions.q: Why did temporary bike lanes become permanent in several cities?
options: Because drivers liked having fewer parking spaces
Because tourists complained about them
Because data showed fewer accidents and better business for local shops
Because removing them was too expensive
questions.explanation: 细节题。第四段末说试行自行车道之所以转为永久，是因为数据显示事故减少、当地商店收入反而增加——用证据说话。

passages.title: The Science of Fear
passages.text:
Standing on a surfboard in front of a wave as tall as a building, or leaping between rooftops with nothing but your own legs to save you, seems — to most people — completely insane. Yet extreme sports such as big-wave surfing, parkour, rock climbing, and BMX riding attract millions of devoted fans, and scientists have become genuinely curious about why anyone would seek fear on purpose.

The answer begins in the brain. In a moment of danger, the body floods with adrenaline and other chemicals that sharpen the senses, quicken reactions, and create a feeling of being intensely alive. Athletes describe a mental state called "flow", in which they become so focused that the rest of the world disappears. Interestingly, brain studies show that the people most attracted to extreme sports are not necessarily careless thrill-seekers; many of them simply have brains that need stronger stimulation to feel fully engaged, and these sports provide it.

What surprises outsiders most is how un-extreme the training actually is. Professional big-wave surfers spend years studying wave patterns and practicing how to survive underwater; top rock climbers check every piece of equipment obsessively; parkour athletes repeat the same jump hundreds of times at ground level before attempting it between rooftops. The goal is never to ignore fear but to understand it. As one famous climber put it, fear is information: it tells you what you have not yet prepared for.

The culture around these sports also challenges an old stereotype. Far from being lone rebels, extreme athletes usually train in tight communities where beginners are carefully mentored, safety knowledge is shared, and showing off is discouraged. Many gyms and city parks now offer parkour classes for teenagers, teaching them to fall safely before teaching them to jump — a skill useful far beyond the sport itself.

There are real dangers, of course, and responsible coaches never pretend otherwise. But researchers note that banning young people from all risk often backfires, pushing them toward riskier behavior without guidance. A well-taught extreme sport may actually be a classroom for courage, judgment, and respect for one's own limits.

So the next time you watch a rider fly over a ramp, remember: what looks like madness from the ground is, up close, a mixture of biology, preparation, and self-knowledge — with just a little bit of madness on top.

questions.q: What is the best title for this passage?
options: The Most Dangerous Sports in the World
How to Become a Professional Surfer
The Science of Fear: Why People Love Extreme Sports
Why Teenagers Should Avoid All Risks
questions.explanation: 主旨题。文章从大脑科学（肾上腺素、心流）和训练文化两方面解释人们为何热爱极限运动，C 准确概括；A、B 只是细节，D 与作者观点相反。

questions.q: In paragraph 3, the word "obsessively" is closest in meaning to ___.
options: quickly and without thinking
only when someone is watching
in a very lazy way
with extreme care and attention, again and again
questions.explanation: 词义猜测题。第三段说顶尖攀岩者检查每一件装备“obsessively”，上下文都在强调训练的严谨细致，可见该词意为“近乎执着地、一丝不苟地”。

questions.q: What is "flow", according to the passage?
options: A wave that is safe for beginners
A mental state of deep focus in which the rest of the world seems to disappear
A kind of safety equipment
A chemical that causes fear
questions.explanation: 细节题。第二段定义了“flow（心流）”：运动员高度专注，以至于外界的一切都仿佛消失的心理状态。

questions.q: According to the passage, professional extreme athletes usually ___.
options: avoid training because it is boring
ignore fear completely
train carefully, repeat moves many times, and respect safety rules
learn everything by themselves without a community
questions.explanation: 细节题。第三段说职业选手多年研究规律、反复检查装备、把同一动作练几百遍，目标不是无视恐惧而是理解恐惧——训练极其严谨、重视安全。

questions.q: What does the famous climber's words — "fear is information" — suggest?
options: Fear tells you what you are not yet prepared for, so you should learn from it
Fear means you should stop the sport immediately
Fear is only felt by beginners
Fear makes athletes perform worse
questions.explanation: 推断题。这句话的意思是：恐惧是一种信号，提醒你哪里还没准备好，应该从中学习、加强准备，而不是简单地逃避或无视它。

passages.title: Will Your First Car Fly?
passages.text:
A century ago, people imagined that by the year 2020 everyone would commute in flying cars. That future arrived late, but engineers are finally catching up with the dream. Three technologies now racing toward reality — magnetic levitation trains, hyperloop systems, and electric air taxis — could completely change how your generation travels.

The most mature of the three is the maglev train. Instead of rolling on wheels, maglev trains float above the track, held up by powerful magnets, which removes almost all friction. Shanghai's airport maglev, running since 2004, reaches 430 kilometers per hour, and newer trains being tested in Japan have exceeded 600. Because they float, these trains are quieter and need less maintenance than ordinary trains; their main weakness is cost, since an entirely new kind of track must be built.

The hyperloop is bolder and stranger. Passengers would sit in capsules traveling through long tubes from which most of the air has been pumped out. With almost no air resistance, capsules could theoretically reach 1,000 kilometers per hour — faster than most airplanes. Several companies have built short test tracks, and volunteers have already ridden prototype capsules, though at much lower speeds. Critics point out enormous challenges: maintaining a near-vacuum across hundreds of kilometers, keeping passengers safe if a tube is damaged, and paying for construction that could cost more than a high-speed railway.

Electric air taxis, sometimes called eVTOLs, are the technology closest to everyday life. These small electric aircraft take off vertically like helicopters but fly far more quietly, using many small rotors instead of one large one. Companies around the world have completed test flights, and several cities hope to launch air-taxi services during major international events in the next few years. A trip that takes an hour in traffic could take ten minutes by air. Before that happens, however, regulators must solve difficult questions about air-traffic control, battery safety, and noise — a sky full of taxis could easily become a sky full of complaints.

Which of these technologies will win? History suggests that the winner is rarely the fastest or the most exciting, but the one that is cheap, safe, and convenient enough for ordinary people to use every day. The bicycle, after all, beat plenty of fancier inventions simply because it worked.

Whatever the outcome, one thing is certain: the transportation system of 2050 is being designed right now, by engineers who were teenagers not long ago — people who grew up, perhaps, playing racing games and wondering when real life would catch up.

questions.q: What is the passage mainly about?
options: Why flying cars will never exist
The history of Shanghai's airport
Three future transportation technologies and what will decide their success
How to build a hyperloop at home
questions.explanation: 主旨题。文章依次介绍磁悬浮、超级高铁和电动空中出租车三项技术，最后讨论决定胜负的因素（便宜、安全、方便），C 概括最全面。

questions.q: How do maglev trains reduce friction?
options: They use special wheels made of rubber
They float above the track, held up by magnets
They travel through vacuum tubes
They fly low over the ground like aircraft
questions.explanation: 细节题。第二段说磁悬浮列车靠强大的磁铁悬浮在轨道上方，不接触轨道，因此几乎消除了摩擦。C 是超级高铁的原理，注意不要混淆。

questions.q: In paragraph 3, a "prototype" is ___.
options: an early model built to test a new design
a product already sold to customers
a type of fuel for capsules
a company that builds trains
questions.explanation: 词义猜测题。第三段说志愿者已乘坐过“prototype”胶囊舱，但速度还低得多，可见 prototype 指用于测试新设计的“原型机/试验型号”。

questions.q: Which of the following is mentioned as a challenge for the hyperloop?
options: Keeping a near-vacuum inside hundreds of kilometers of tube
Finding enough passengers to fill the capsules
Training pilots to drive the capsules
Making the capsules slow enough to stop
questions.explanation: 细节题。第三段末列举了超级高铁的挑战：在数百公里管道中维持接近真空、管道受损时的乘客安全，以及高昂的建造成本。A 与原文对应。

questions.q: The example of the bicycle in paragraph 5 suggests that ___.
options: bicycles will replace airplanes in the future
the most successful technology is usually the practical and affordable one, not the most exciting one
old inventions are always better than new ones
engineers should stop inventing new things
questions.explanation: 推断题。作者说历史上胜出的往往不是最快、最炫的技术，而是便宜、安全、方便、普通人天天用得起的，自行车正是靠“好用”击败了无数花哨发明。
